fields.label: Connection string
fields.description: A connection string contains the parameters required to connect to MongoDB.

help.title: Connection string
help.markdown: A connection string contains the parameters required to connect to MongoDB. [View formatting details here.](https://www.mongodb.com/docs/manual/reference/connection-string/)

fields.label: Authentication method
fields.description: Choose an authentication method to access the data source

options.label: No Authentication
options.description: Data source is available without authentication

options.label: Credentials
options.description: Authenticate with default credentials assigned to the MongoDB account upon creation.

options.label: Kerberos
options.description: Authenticate with Kerberos. Requires connection string to include `authMethod=GSSAPI` as a query parameter.

fields.label: Basic authentication enabled
fields.description: Standard Grafana basic-auth enabled flag. The backend initializes BasicAuthEnabled from this value and also forces it on when a username or password is present (pkg/models/settings.go). Set by datasource provisioning and by the config editor's on-load migration of legacy datasources, not by selecting the Credentials method in the current editor.

fields.label: User
fields.description: The username assigned to the MongoDB account.

fields.label: Password
fields.description: The password assigned to the MongoDB account.

fields.label: User
fields.description: The client principal's username. Enabled when connection string includes query string authMethod=GSSAPI

fields.label: Password
fields.description: The client principal password that will be used to authenticate. Optional if a keytab or cache file is present.

fields.label: KeyTab file path
fields.description: Absolute file path KeyTab for keytab file. If present will ignore password. Enabled when connection string includes query string authMethod=GSSAPI

fields.label: Global ccache file path
fields.description: Absolute file path to global compiler cache (ccache) file. If present will ignore password. Enabled when connection string includes query string authMethod=GSSAPI

fields.label: Ccache lookup file
fields.description: Absolute file path to  the JSON file that provides the Kerberos compiler cache (ccache) based on username principal and connection string. If present will ignore password. Enabled when connection string includes query string authMethod=GSSAPI

fields.label: Enable syntax validation
fields.description: Enable real time query syntax validation. MongoDB BSON syntax will be validated as you type and show contextual errors.

fields.label: Password
fields.description: Password

fields.label: Rows to Return
fields.description: Increasing this too much may lead to performance issues for larger queries
TLS server name used to verify the hostname on the server's certificate when tlsAuth is enabled. Not exposed in the configuration editor; set via datasource provisioning.
Enables TLS client-certificate authentication; the backend supplies tlsClientCert and tlsClientKey to the server. Not exposed in the configuration editor; set via datasource provisioning.
Enables verification of the server's TLS certificate against a custom CA (tlsCACert). Not exposed in the configuration editor; set via datasource provisioning.

Skips verification of the server's TLS certificate chain and host name (applied by the backend when tlsAuthWithCACert is enabled). Not exposed in the configuration editor; set via datasource provisioning.
CA certificate PEM used to verify the server's TLS certificate when tlsAuthWithCACert is enabled. Not exposed in the configuration editor; set via datasource provisioning.
Client certificate PEM used when tlsAuth is enabled. Not exposed in the configuration editor; set via datasource provisioning.
Client private key PEM used when tlsAuth is enabled. Not exposed in the configuration editor; set via datasource provisioning.

Legacy username field. Datasources created before v1.9.0 stored the MongoDB username here; the backend migrates it to the root basicAuthUser and enables basic auth (pkg/models/settings.go). New configurations use the root basicAuthUser field.
Legacy flag; the backend copies it to tlsSkipVerify (InsecureSkipVerify) at load time (pkg/models/settings.go). New configurations use tlsSkipVerify.
Legacy frontend-only flag used by the config editor's on-load migration to detect pre-v1.9.0 basic-auth datasources. Never read by the backend.

Legacy secure password. Datasources created before v1.9.0 stored the MongoDB password here; the backend migrates it to the basic-auth password (pkg/models/settings.go). New configurations use basicAuthPassword.

groups.title: Additional Settings
groups.description: Additional settings are optional settings that can be configured for more control over your data source.

relationships.description: Kerberos credential inputs (used when authType is custom-Kerberos and the connection string includes authMechanism=GSSAPI)
TLS client-certificate authentication: enable flag plus the client certificate and key
Custom-CA server verification toggle and its CA certificate
Legacy basic-auth username/password pair migrated to root basicAuthUser + secure basicAuthPassword

instructions.msg: Three authentication methods are available, selected via jsonData.authType: 'NoAuth', 'BasicAuth' (the default, labelled 'Credentials' in the editor), and 'custom-Kerberos'. If authType is empty or unrecognized the backend coerces it to 'BasicAuth'. jsonData.connection (the MongoDB connection string) is required for every method.

To configure Credentials (basic auth), set jsonData.authType='BasicAuth', put the username in the ROOT-level basicAuthUser field (not jsonData) and the password in secureJsonData.basicAuthPassword. Minimal payload: {"basicAuth":true,"basicAuthUser":"<user>","jsonData":{"authType":"BasicAuth","connection":"mongodb://host:27017/db"},"secureJsonData":{"basicAuthPassword":"<password>"}}. The backend injects the credentials into the connection string as 'user:password@'; percent-encode any reserved characters in the username/password (or embed them, already encoded, directly in the connection string).

To configure Kerberos, set jsonData.authType='custom-Kerberos' and include 'authMechanism=GSSAPI' as a query parameter in jsonData.connection (Kerberos is only activated when the connection string contains authMechanism=GSSAPI AND jsonData.kerberosUser is set). Provide jsonData.kerberosUser and one credential source: secureJsonData.kerberosPassword, or jsonData.keyTabFilePath, jsonData.globalCcacheFilePath, or jsonData.ccacheLookupFile (a file path takes precedence over the password). Minimal payload: {"jsonData":{"authType":"custom-Kerberos","connection":"mongodb://host:27017/?authMechanism=GSSAPI","kerberosUser":"user@REALM"},"secureJsonData":{"kerberosPassword":"<password>"}}. Kerberos requires a custom plugin build and is not available on Grafana Cloud.

Legacy interpretation: datasources created before v1.9.0 stored the username in jsonData.user and the password in secureJsonData.password. On load the backend copies these into the basic-auth username/password and enables basic auth. The modern root basicAuthUser and secureJsonData.basicAuthPassword override the legacy values when both are present. Do not treat a missing basicAuthUser as unconfigured if jsonData.user is set.

secureJsonData values (basicAuthPassword, kerberosPassword, tlsCertificateKeyFilePassword, tlsCACert, tlsClientCert, tlsClientKey, password) are write-only: they can be set or reset but never read back. To check whether a secret is already configured on an existing datasource, read the secureJsonFields object (e.g. secureJsonFields.basicAuthPassword == true) instead of secureJsonData.

TLS is configured through provisioning only (the config editor exposes no TLS certificate fields): set jsonData.tlsAuth=true for client-certificate auth (supply secureJsonData.tlsClientCert and secureJsonData.tlsClientKey, plus secureJsonData.tlsCertificateKeyFilePassword if the key is encrypted, and optionally jsonData.serverName), and jsonData.tlsAuthWithCACert=true to verify the server against a custom CA (supply secureJsonData.tlsCACert). tls* certificate/key values are read from secureJsonData. If the connection string contains an '@' in the credentials, percent-encode it (e.g. p@ss becomes p%40ss).
